fact: June 25 sits exactly 176 days into the year, which means there are only 189 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this date falls just 4 days after the summer solstice, so you're still getting some of the longest daylight hours of the entire year.
Fireflies typically reach peak flashing activity around this time in North America - they blink their butts off trying to find mates in the warm summer nights.
In Antarctica, June 25 is smack in the middle of their polar night season where it's dark for 24 hours straight.
Strawberry plants in many northern regions are usually producing their juiciest, reddest berries right around this date.
June 25 falls under the Cancer zodiac sign - represented by a crab that carries its home on its back.
In northern countries like Alaska and northern Canada, the sun might set for only 2-3 hours around this date, creating an eerie twilight that lasts almost all night.
Mosquitoes are typically at their most annoying peak right around now in many places because the warm, humid weather creates perfect breeding conditions.
Rose bushes often hit their spectacular blooming peak in late June in temperate climates, with gardens bursting with colorful, fragrant flowers.
Ocean water temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere are usually getting warm enough for comfortable swimming without a wetsuit around this time of year.
When there's a full moon on June 25, it appears lower in the sky than winter full moons because of Earth's tilt.
